dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for heart problems/AICD.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
Today, I started to have anterior chest pain. It's a 5 out of 10, sharp, and it radiates to my back.
You seem alert. Do you know the date and what today is?
Yes, today is Tuesday.
You're opening your eyes spontaneously.
You're responding appropriately, with full orientation.
Right-side movement is limited-can you squeeze my hand with that hand?
It's weak, but I can manage.
You are breathing independently and have no signs of airway compromise.
You're breathing 20 times per minute, and your breaths are regular. Your oxygen saturation is 99% on room air.
Let me check your pulse and skin. Pulse is 91, and your skin is pink and warm.
Pulse is 91 beats per minute. Blood pressure is 160 over 91.
Oxygen saturation is 99% on room air.
Blood glucose is 104 mg/dL.
I'm now placing you on a 4-lead ECG monitor.
EKG is showing sinus rhythm.
I'll now perform a 12-lead ECG for further evaluation.
Since you're experiencing chest pain, I'm giving you 324 mg of aspirin to chew. This is protocol for suspected cardiac pain.
Let's go over your allergies, medications, and health history to be thorough.
I'm allergic to codeine, Percocet 10/325, and Humalog Mix.
What medications do you take on a regular basis?
Aspirin, Baclofen, Clonidine Hydrochloride, famotidine, loratadine, Metoprolol, mycophenolate mofetil, Pravastatin, Prednisone, sevelamer, tacrolimus, and tamsulosin.
Can you recall any relevant medical history we should know about?
I've had a stroke, resulting in right-side weakness. I also have high blood pressure and rely on dialysis for kidney function.
What time did you have something to eat or drink last?
I had a meal in the morning before my dialysis session today.
The chest pain started this morning. Has it been consistent or did it vary?
The pain is constant but seems to increase a little with positions or movement.
The pain is sharp and it radiates to your back. That is often consistent with cardiac pain.
Yes, it moves from the front of my chest to my upper back.
Your latest blood pressure is 141 over 72.
Pulse is now 84 beats per minute.
Oxygen saturation is 97% on room air.
Second EKG also shows sinus rhythm.
The second glucose reading couldn't be obtained-result is zero.
Skin is still warm, pink, and dry with no notable rashes or wounds.
Your head and neck exams are normal, with no swelling or deformity. Pupils are equal and reactive.
Your nose and throat appear normal; no signs of obstruction.
Chest rise is equal. No signs of deformity or abnormal movements.
Your abdomen is flat, with no tenderness or distension. Bowel sounds intact.
Peripheral pulses are strong and evenly distributed. No deformities in your limbs.
Respiratory rate remains stable at 20 per minute.
Oxygen saturation is 97%, still in sinus rhythm. No immediate changes.
On a scale from 1 to 10, where would you currently rate your chest pain?
Still a 5, not worsening at all.
Any new changes to symptoms or medical condition?
No new changes. Still on all my regular medications.
We're now securing you on the stretcher and fastening for transport to the ambulance.
We will be transporting you to VCU Health Systems for further evaluation.
Final vitals are 146/83, heart rate 80, 20 breaths, and oxygen at 98%. Still in sinus rhythm.
Based on findings, our field impression is chest pain with concern for cardiac cause and history of neurological deficit due to a prior stroke and CVA. We are following the chest pain protocol.
All assessment data, interventions, and EKG traces have been documented and shared with the ED nurse. They are ready to receive the patient.
We've transferred you to the stretcher and applied a five-point harness. All seat belts are attached.
EMS crew has confirmed all medical protocol was followed and patient met all transport criteria.
EKG was transmitted, and the ED is aware of clotting procedures in place due to your CVA history.
We conducted transport using the stretcher transfer method, sliding the patient onto the ED bed as per protocol.
Patient care was officially transferred to the ED nurse after confirming consent and signature.
Field impression retains concern for cardiac involvement. Transport was per protocol, including all required monitoring and interventions.
All four-lead and 12-lead EKGs are documented and properly interpreted in field as per standing order.
Aspirin was administered correctly due to elevated risk profile and suspected cardiac cause.
Throat examination reveals healthy mucous membranes-no signs of infection or secretion.
Peripheral pulses were assessed as strong and regular, consistent with clear cardiac output.
Right-side weakness confirmed during neuro exam, consistent with hemiplegia post CVA.
You mentioned the pain is a 5-consistently sharp and radiating to the back. No change in nature.
No new associated symptoms with pain, such as sweating or dizziness reported.
Your usual dialysis is being done three times a week, with the session ongoing today.
Your normal medication regimen appears consistent, and you've taken all scheduled doses.
No reported trauma or injury in the hours leading up to the event or contributing to current condition.
Final EKG showed no repolarization abnormalities and maintained sinus rhythm throughout the shift.
All assets and personal effects of the patient remain undisturbed and securely stowed as we proceed.
Disembarkation from scene followed controlled sequence with no patient instability observed during movement.
Transport time was minimized as per protocol to reduce patient exposure to environmental conditions.
Repeated four-lead monitoring reinforced ongoing assessments as per field protocol for cardiac concerns.
Wrist and hand areas-no signs of digital cyanosis or swelling.
Skin texture is intact, with no evidence of breakdown.
Second and final 12-lead EKG recorded for archive and comparison in ED.
No signs of petechiae or purpura consistent with clotting disorders or CVA-related hypotension episodes.
Dialysis session was ongoing at time of emergency call-no acute changes reported this morning.
No significant or progressive drop in pulse rate or rhythm, consistent throughout shift.
Vitals were rechecked three times-any changes were documented and consistent with stable condition.
Shoulder straps and lap harness both engaged for safe transport despite no trauma noted.
All steps taken to move on stretcher excessively cautious due to neuro deficit potentially increasing intra-cranial pressure.
Chronic care considerations of dialysis and CVA history remained constant during entire assessment.
All findings were normal and consistent with stable baseline, with no interventions required beyond protocol.
EMS has now completed their assessment and exited per protocol to ED care transfer.
